entries.content: We need to prepare the OBEY project for the Synthesis hackathon. I want to target Protocol Labs, Uniswap, Status Network, Markee, and Open Track bounties. Create a festival to plan this out.

Created synthesis-submission-SS0001 festival with 6 sequences: verify Uniswap API, generate Protocol Labs artifacts, mainnet deployment, low-effort bounties, repo cleanup, and submission packaging. 53 tasks total across the IMPLEMENT phase.
The ritual agent-market-research-RI-AM0001 already has fully customized WORKFLOW.md files. We don't need to build new systems — just wire ob fest execute into the synthesis festival tasks.

Updated 03_run_research_ritual.md to use ob fest execute --festival <run-id> instead of manual fest next instructions. Each ritual run will be driven autonomously end-to-end. Deleted the fake ritual run artifacts from earlier.
Why would we run the ritual now? That's part of the implementation — there's no reason to run it until we have our code created that actually runs it.

You're right. The ritual executes code that doesn't exist yet — the API client, the executor integration. Running the ritual before that code exists would just fail. Marked it blocked and moved to the next task.
Does task 04 (generate_agent_log) actually need the ritual to have run? Don't we just need to create an easy way for the agent to find the data it needs?

Exactly right. Built cmd/loggen — a Go tool that queries on-chain SwapExecuted events from the vault contract and walks ritual artifact directories for agent_log_entry.json files. The pipeline exists; when data is available, just run: just loggen -rituals <dir> -out agent_log.json

Wrote two differentiated narratives for Protocol Labs: 'Let the Agent Cook' focuses on autonomous multi-tool orchestration and the festival loop. 'Agents With Receipts' focuses on ERC-8004 identity, on-chain verifiability, and trust building. Both 300-500 words, genuinely distinct angles on the same project.

Code review found 2 critical issues in loggen: duplicate WETH map entry and fmt.Errorf usage violating project standards. Also found iter.Error() not checked after event loop, and hard-coded chain values. Fixed all issues — builds clean, vet passes.
Don't deploy to mainnet. Just testnet — we can do mainnet after everything has been built and tested.

Makes sense. Skipped mainnet deployment sequence. Moved to low-effort bounties. Successfully deployed AgentIdentityRegistry to Status Network Sepolia (gasless chain) and registered the OBEY agent identity in a second gasless transaction.

Secrets audit complete. All .env files are gitignored across parent repo and all submodules. No secrets in git history. Zero exposure risk when repos go public. Documented testnet keys that should be rotated post-hackathon.
Submodule audit: 6 Blockhead-Consulting repos are private (404), 3 submodules use local paths. Only cre-risk-router and forge-std are publicly accessible. Documented all findings with action items.
They will eventually be public but not yet. Don't make any repo public — there is no reason to.
Understood. The audit is documented so you know exactly which repos to flip when ready. No visibility changes made.
